title: Match equations to coordinates on a graph
description: Identify the coordinates that match a two-variable equation. Also, identify the corresponding equation when given  coordinates.
topic_path: Match equations to coordinates on a graph

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the relationship between the $x$- and $y$- coordinates on the following graph?** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graph.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can  check all $3$ sets of ordered pairs to see if they match each equation.


Equation: $\greenD y=\blueD3\maroonD x$


$\begin{align} \greenD3 & \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD3\cdot \maroonD 1\\\\
\greenD6 & \neq\blueD3\cdot\maroonD 4 \\\\
\greenD9 &\neq\blueD3\cdot\maroonD 7 \end{align}$



Equation: $\greenD y=\maroonD x\goldD{+2}$



$\begin{align} \greenD 3&\stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 1\goldD{+2} \\\\
\greenD 6 & \stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 4\goldD{+2} \\\\
\greenD 9 & \stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 7\goldD{+2}\end{align}$
The equation that works for all $3$ sets of ordered pairs is $y=x+2$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1.  Solid points are plotted at (1, 3), (4, 6), and (7, 9).

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the relationship between the $x$- and $y$- coordinates on the following graph?** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graph.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can  check all $3$ sets of ordered pairs to see if they match each equation.


Equation: $\greenD y=\maroonD x \blueD{+1}$


$\begin{align} \greenD3 & \stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 2 \blueD{+1}\\\\
\greenD6 &\stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 5 \blueD{+1} \\\\
\greenD8 &\stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 7 \blueD{+1} \end{align}$

Equation: $\greenD y=\maroonD x\goldD{-1}$



$\begin{align} \greenD 3&\neq\maroonD 2\goldD{-1} \\\\
\greenD 6 & \neq\maroonD 5\goldD{-1} \\\\
\greenD 8 & \neq\maroonD 7\goldD{-1}\end{align}$
The equation that works for all $3$ sets of ordered pairs is $y=x+1$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1.  Solid points are plotted at (2, 3), (5, 6), and (7, 8).

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the relationship between the $x$- and $y$- coordinates on the following graph?** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graph.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can  check all $3$ sets of ordered pairs to see if they match each equation.


Equation: $\greenD y=\maroonD x $


$\begin{align} \greenD2 & \stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 2 \\\\
\greenD5 &\stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 5  \\\\
\greenD8 &\stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 8 \end{align}$

Equation: $\greenD y=\blueD2\maroonD x\goldD{-2}$

$\begin{align} \greenD 2&\stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2\cdot\maroonD 2\goldD{-2} \\\\
\greenD 5 & \neq\blueD2 \cdot\maroonD 5\goldD{-2} \\\\
\greenD 8 & \neq\blueD2 \cdot\maroonD 8\goldD{-2}\end{align}$
The equation that works for all $3$ sets of ordered pairs is $y=x$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1.  Solid points are plotted at (2, 2), (5, 5), and (8, 8).

choices.content: $y=x$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the relationship between the $x$- and $y$- coordinates on the following graph?** 


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graph.



[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can  check all $3$ sets of ordered pairs to see if they match each equation.

Equation: $\greenD y=\blueD2\maroonD x $

$\begin{align} \greenD0 & \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2 \cdot\maroonD 0 \\\\
\greenD4 &\stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2 \cdot \maroonD 2  \\\\
\greenD8 &\stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2\cdot\maroonD 4 \end{align}$

Equation: $\greenD y=\maroonD x\goldD{+2}$

$\begin{align} \greenD 0&\neq\maroonD 0\goldD{+2} \\\\
\greenD 4 & \stackrel\checkmark=\maroonD 2\goldD{+2} \\\\
\greenD 8 & \neq\maroonD 4\goldD{+2}\end{align}$
The equation that works for all $3$ sets of ordered pairs is $y=2x$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1.  Solid points are plotted at (0, 0), (2, 4), and (4, 8).

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the relationship between the $x$- and $y$- coordinates on the following graph?** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graph.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can  check all $3$ sets of ordered pairs to see if they match each equation.

Equation: $\greenD y=\blueD3\maroonD x $

$\begin{align} \greenD2 & \neq\blueD3 \cdot\maroonD 0 \\\\
\greenD6 &\stackrel\checkmark=\blueD3 \cdot \maroonD 2  \\\\
\greenD8&\neq  \blueD3\cdot\maroonD 3 \end{align}$

Equation: $\greenD y=\blueD2\maroonD x\goldD{+2}$

$\begin{align} \greenD 2& \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2 \cdot\maroonD 0\goldD{+2} \\\\
\greenD 6 & \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2 \cdot\maroonD 2\goldD{+2} \\\\
\greenD 8 & \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2 \cdot \maroonD 3\goldD{+2}\end{align}$
The equation that works for all $3$ sets of ordered pairs is $y=2x + 2$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. Solid points are plotted at (0, 2), (2, 6), and (3, 8).

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the relationship between the $x$- and $y$- coordinates on the following graph?** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graph.



[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can  check all $3$ sets of ordered pairs to see if they match each equation.

Equation: $\greenD y=\maroonD x \goldD{+1} $

$\begin{align} \greenD3 &\stackrel\checkmark= \maroonD 2 \goldD{+1}\\\\
\greenD5 &\neq \maroonD 3 \goldD{+1} \\\\
\greenD7 &\neq  \maroonD 4 \goldD{+1} \end{align}$

Equation: $\greenD y=\blueD2\maroonD x\goldD{-1}$

$\begin{align} \greenD 3& \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2 \cdot\maroonD 2\goldD{-1} \\\\
\greenD 5 & \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2 \cdot\maroonD 3\goldD{-1} \\\\
\greenD 7 & \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD2 \cdot \maroonD 4\goldD{-1}\end{align}$
The equation that works for all $3$ sets of ordered pairs is $y=2x -1$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1.  Solid points are plotted at (2, 3), (3, 5), and (4, 7).

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the relationship between the $x$- and $y$- coordinates on the following graph?** 


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graph.



[[☃ image 1]]

Equation: $\greenD y=\blueD{0.5}\maroonD x\goldD{+3}$



$\begin{align} \greenD 3& \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD{0.5} \cdot\maroonD 0\goldD{+3} \\\\
\greenD 5 & \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD{0.5} \cdot\maroonD 4\goldD{+3} \\\\
\greenD 7 & \stackrel\checkmark=\blueD{0.5} \cdot \maroonD 8\goldD{+3}\end{align}$

Now we can  check all $3$ sets of ordered pairs to see if they match each equation.

Equation: $\greenD y=\maroonD x \goldD{+3}$

$\begin{align} \greenD3 & \stackrel\checkmark= \maroonD 0 \goldD{+3} \\\\
\greenD5 &\neq \maroonD 4 \goldD{+3}  \\\\
\greenD7 &\neq  \maroonD 8 \goldD{+3} \end{align}$
The equation that works for all $3$ sets of ordered pairs is $y=0.5x + 3$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1.  Solid points are plotted at (0, 3), (4, 5), and (8, 7).

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 0.5x$ true?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graphs. 

Then we can substitute the ordered pairs for each graph into the equation $\redD y=0.5\purpleC x$ to see if the equation represents that graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y} &=0.5\purpleC{x}\\\\
\redD {0} &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=}{0.5} \cdot \purpleC 0 \\\\
\redD {5} &\neq{0.5} \cdot \purpleC 5 \\\\
\redD{8} &\neq{0.5} \cdot\purpleC 8
\end{align}$

Some of the coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y} &=0.5\purpleC{x}\\\\
\redD {5} &\neq{0.5} \cdot \purpleC {2} \\\\
\redD {7} &\neq{0.5} \cdot \purpleC {4} \\\\
\redD{ 9} &\neq{0.5} \cdot \purpleC {6}
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y} &=0.5\purpleC{x}\\\\
\redD {2} &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} {0.5} \cdot \purpleC {4} \\\\
\redD {3} &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} {0.5}  \cdot \purpleC {6} \\\\
\redD{ 4} &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} {0.5} \cdot \purpleC {8}
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do* make the equation true.

The following graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 0.5x$ true.



[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1.  Solid points are plotted at (0, 0), (5, 5), and (8, 8).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3820ff0ae0ca7af8c6a057003a133f8df7ea0fa5)

![A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1.  Solid points are plotted at (2, 5), (4, 7), and (6, 9).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6fcdbd66dedc6ec546544173a3c1424d3243e027)

![A coordinate plane. The x-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1. The y-axis runs from 0 to 11 and is scaled by 1.  Solid points are plotted at (4, 2), (6, 3), and (8, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d760b70a411cd9569bf6e7160e58a3b05ca71d09)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = x+3$ true?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graphs. 

Then we can substitute the ordered pairs for each graph into the equation $\redD {y}=\purpleC {x}+3$ to see if the equation represents that graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=\purpleC {x}+3 \\\\
\redD {3}&\neq \purpleC {1}+3 \\\\
\redD {6}&\neq \purpleC {2}+3 \\\\
\redD {9}&\neq \purpleC {3}+3
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=\purpleC {x}+3 \\\\
\redD {4}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= \purpleC {1}+3 \\\\
\redD {6}&\stackrel{\checkmark}=\purpleC {3}+3 \\\\
\redD {9}&\stackrel{\checkmark}=\purpleC {6}+3
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=\purpleC {x}+3 \\\\
\redD {1}&\neq \purpleC {0}+3 \\\\
\redD {4}&\neq \purpleC {4}+3 \\\\
\redD {7}&\neq \purpleC {8}+3
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

The following graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = x+3$ true.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 2x$ true?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graphs. 

Then we can substitute the ordered pairs for each graph into the equation $\redD y=2\purpleC x$ to see if the equation represents that graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=2\purpleC {x} \\\\
\redD {4}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 2 \cdot \purpleC {2} \\\\
\redD {8}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 2\cdot \purpleC {4} \\\\
\redD {10}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 2\cdot \purpleC {5} 
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=2\purpleC {x} \\\\
\redD {2}&\neq 2 \cdot \purpleC {0} \\\\
\redD {3}&\neq 2 \cdot \purpleC {4} \\\\
\redD {4}&\neq 2 \cdot \purpleC {8} 
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=2\purpleC {x} \\\\
\redD {6}&\neq 2 \cdot \purpleC {2} \\\\
\redD {5}&\neq 2\cdot \purpleC {4} \\\\
\redD {3}&\neq 2\cdot \purpleC {8} 
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

The following graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 2x$ true.


[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = x-1$ true?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graphs. 

Then we can substitute the ordered pairs for each graph into the equation $\redD y=\purpleC x -1$ to see if the equation represents that graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=\purpleC {x} -1\\\\
\redD {1}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= \purpleC {2} -1\\\\
\redD {4}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= \purpleC {5} -1\\\\
\redD {7}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= \purpleC {8} -1
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=\purpleC {x} -1\\\\
\redD {2}&\neq \purpleC {0} -1\\\\
\redD {3}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= \purpleC {4} -1\\\\
\redD {4}&\neq \purpleC {8} -1
\end{align}$

Some of the coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=\purpleC {x} -1\\\\
\redD {6}&\neq \purpleC {2} -1\\\\
\redD {8}&\neq \purpleC {6} -1\\\\
\redD {10}&\neq \purpleC {10} -1
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

The following graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = x-1$ true.


[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 2x+1$ true?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graphs. 

Then we can substitute the ordered pairs for each graph into the equation $\redD y=2\purpleC x+1$ to see if the equation represents that graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=2  \purpleC {x} +1\\\\
\redD {1}&\neq 2  \cdot\purpleC {4} +1\\\\
\redD {3}&\neq 2  \cdot\purpleC  {8} +1\\\\
\redD {4}&\neq 2  \cdot\purpleC  {10} +1
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=2  \purpleC {x} +1\\\\
\redD {3}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 2 \cdot \purpleC {1} +1\\\\
\redD {7}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 2 \cdot \purpleC  {3} +1\\\\
\redD {9}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 2 \cdot \purpleC {4} +1
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=2  \purpleC {x} +1\\\\
\redD {6}&\neq 2 \cdot \purpleC {2} +1\\\\
\redD {8}&\neq 2 \cdot \purpleC  {6} +1\\\\
\redD {9}&\neq 2 \cdot \purpleC {8} +1
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

The following graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 2x+1$ true.


[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 3x-2$ true?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graphs. 

Then we can substitute the ordered pairs for each graph into the equation $\redD y=3\purpleC x-2$ to see if the equation represents that graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=3\purpleC {x} -2\\\\
\redD {2}&\neq 3 \cdot \purpleC {4} -2\\\\
\redD {5}&\neq 3 \cdot \purpleC {7} -2\\\\
\redD {7}&\neq 3 \cdot \purpleC {9} -2
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=3\purpleC {x} -2\\\\
\redD {1}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 3 \cdot \purpleC {1} -2\\\\
\redD {4}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 3 \cdot \purpleC {2} -2\\\\
\redD {10}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 3 \cdot \purpleC {4} -2
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=3\purpleC {x} -2\\\\
\redD {6}&\neq 3 \cdot \purpleC {2} -2\\\\
\redD {7}&\stackrel{\checkmark}= 3 \cdot \purpleC {3} -2\\\\
\redD {8}&\neq 3 \cdot \purpleC {6} -2
\end{align}$

Some of the coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

The following graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 3x-2$ true.


[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 0.5x+5$ true?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First let's assign an ordered pair to each point shown in the graphs. 

Then we can substitute the ordered pairs for each graph into the equation $\redD y=0.5\purpleC x+5$ to see if the equation represents that graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=0.5\purpleC {x} +1\\\\
\redD {5}&\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 0.5 \cdot \purpleC {0} +5\\\\
\redD {2}&\neq 0.5 \cdot \purpleC {4} +5\\\\
\redD {3}&\neq 0.5 \cdot \purpleC {8} +5
\end{align}$

Some of the coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=0.5\purpleC {x} +1\\\\
\redD {0}&\neq 0.5 \cdot \purpleC {1} +5\\\\
\redD {4}&\neq 0.5 \cdot \purpleC {3} +5\\\\
\redD {10}&\neq 0.5 \cdot \purpleC {6} +5
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do not* make the equation true.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
\redD {y}&=0.5\purpleC {x} +1\\\\
\redD {6}&\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 0.5 \cdot \purpleC {2} +5\\\\
\redD {7}&\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 0.5 \cdot \purpleC {4} +5\\\\
\redD {8}&\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 0.5 \cdot \purpleC {6} +5
\end{align}$

The coordinate pairs on this graph *do* make the equation true.

The following graph represents $(x,y)$-pairs that make the equation $y = 0.5x+5$ true.



[[☃ image 1]]
